sample_tweets.text: 📍“ALIEN ATTACK — HUMANS, WE HAVE ARRIVED.”  A mysterious emergency alert was suddenly sent to smartphones across Brazil through the country’s Civil Defense warning system.  The alert displayed a possible tornado warning near Belo Horizonte, but the message itself read:  “ATAQUE ALIENÍGENA, HUMANOS CHEGAMOS”  “ALIEN ATTACK — HUMANS, WE HAVE ARRIVED.”  Brazilian authorities said the system had been accessed without authorization and temporarily suspended the alert platform.  The Federal Police are now investigating the incident.  #Brazil #AlienAttack #Aliens #UFO #UAP #EmergencyAlert #Hacking #CyberAttack #CivilDefense

📍「宇宙人の攻撃――人間たちよ、我々は到着した」  ブラジル各地のスマートフォンに、国家防災警報システムから突然送信された謎の緊急警報。  動画では「ベロオリゾンテ周辺で竜巻の可能性」と表示される一方、本文には、  「ATAQUE ALIENÍGENA, HUMANOS CHEGAMOS」 「宇宙人の攻撃、人間たちよ、我々は到着した」  という異様なメッセージが表示されている。  ブラジル政府はシステムへの不正侵入があったとして、警報プラットフォームを停止。 現在、連邦警察が捜査している。  #ブラジル #宇宙人 #エイリアン #緊急警報 #ハッキング #サイバー攻撃 #UFO #UAP #Brazil #AlienAttack #Aliens #CyberAttack

🚨'Humans, we have arrived!' Brazilians receive alien invasion alerts  Residents across Brazil were woken up by emergency alerts sent through the country's Civil Defense system. In Belo Horizonte, one message reportedly warned of a tornado before adding:  "Protect yourself: ALIEN ATTACK. Humans, we have arrived."  Other people received an 'extreme alert' containing the word misanthropy, meaning hatred or distrust of humanity. These messages arrived through channels associated with the same national warning system used for floods, storms, landslides and other genuine emergencies.  Obviously there was no alien invasion, although I imagine a few people briefly reconsidered every life decision they had ever made while staring at their phones in the middle of the night.  Brazil's government says the platform was remotely activated by someone outside the National Protection and Civil Defense System. The service was taken offline at around 1:30 a.m. on Saturday, June 20, and the Federal Police are now investigating what officials believe was a cyberattack.  The alien message is obviously funny, but the aliens did not invade Brazil, someone invaded Brazil's emergency alert system and thought that extraterrestrial disclosure would be hilarious.   #Brazil #AlienAttack #UAP #UFO #Disclosure #CivilDefense #CyberAttack #Extraterrestrial
#CyberAttack

First: fix the factual spine  The alert incident is real, but the post needs precision. Brazil’s Defesa Civil Alerta system sent false alerts between the night of Friday, June 19, and early Saturday, June 20, 2026. Agência Brasil reported that the false alerts reached at least seven states plus the Federal District, in cities including Belo Horizonte, Brasília, Campo Grande, Curitiba, Rio Branco, Rio de Janeiro, Salvador and São Paulo; those cities together total about 30 million people. The government said 10 false notifications were sent: nine via Cell Broadcast and one by SMS. Some messages reportedly mentioned “misantropia” and “invasão alienígena,” among other terms.  Call it Brazil’s national mobile emergency alert system, not a generic “national emergency broadcast system.” Anatel describes Defesa Civil Alerta as a Cell Broadcast tool that pushes emergency pop-up messages directly to compatible 4G/5G phones in disaster-risk areas, interrupting whatever is on screen. It was rolled out nationally by October 2025.  The government explanation is also specific: Reuters reported that Brazil suspected a hacking attack, that the notification system was taken offline around 1:30 a.m. local time, and that the unauthorized alert was referred to the Federal Police for investigation. Agência Brasil reported that Anatel is also investigating and that preliminary information suggested the alerts did not pass through the official technical platform operated by ABR Telecom.  The World Cup part is also real: FIFA’s own schedule lists Scotland vs Brazil at Miami Stadium on June 24, 2026. Viral reporting says a Brazilian spiritualist known as Vo Bahiana predicted a UFO/mothership event or abduction scenario at that match; reports describe her as having over 23 million Instagram followers, but the claim remains a social-media prophecy, not evidence.  So the safest correction is:  A real hacked-alert incident happened. A real viral alien-abduction prophecy exists. A real Brazil match is scheduled in Miami on June 24. What is not proven is that these are connected, coordinated, predictive or part of Operation Blue Beam.  Best upgraded version of the post  Something very strange just happened in Brazil — but the real story may be information warfare, not aliens.Overnight from June 19 to June 20, Brazil’s Defesa Civil Alerta system sent false emergency alerts to millions of people across multiple states. Some alerts reportedly used words like “misantropia” and “invasão alienígena,” and local reports from Belo Horizonte described messages saying “Proteja-se: ataque alienígena. Humanos, chegamos.”Authorities say the system was likely compromised and have referred the incident to the Federal Police. The platform was taken offline while investigators examine whether this was one person, a coordinated group, or a wider breach.On its own, that is already serious. Emergency alert systems are supposed to be the most trusted channel a government has. If people start treating them as hackable joke machines, the next flood, fire, landslide or real disaster becomes more dangerous.But the timing is what makes this explode online. Days earlier, a Brazilian spiritualist with a massive following went viral for predicting that UFOs would appear and abduct people at the June 24 Brazil vs Scotland World Cup match in Miami.So now you have:a hacked national alert system, alien-invasion language, a viral psychic prediction, Brazil playing in Miami, World Cup-level attention, and an internet already primed for UAP disclosure and Blue Beam narratives.That does not prove Operation Blue Beam.It proves the conditions for a mass perception event are already here.The question is not only “are aliens coming?”The question is: who benefits from making millions of people distrust emergency alerts, expect spectacle, and interpret every anomaly through an alien-invasion script?  More aggressive version  Brazil may have just shown us what a modern panic rehearsal looks like — whether intentional or not.A national emergency alert system is compromised. Messages mention misanthropy and alien invasion. A viral psychic has already warned of mass abductions at Brazil’s June 24 World Cup match in Miami. Social media instantly stitches the two together as Operation Blue Beam.Maybe it is just a hacker stunt. Maybe it is a prank. Maybe it is psychological vandalism. Maybe it is a test of public reaction. Maybe it is pure coincidence.But the serious issue is this: the infrastructure for mass belief manipulation no longer requires holograms in the sky.It requires trusted alerts, social media, a global sporting event, a viral prophecy, screenshots, fear, memes, and millions of people trying to decode the pattern in real time.Blue Beam may be the wrong theory. But mass perception engineering is very real.  Short viral version  Brazil did not prove Blue Beam.But Brazil just showed the recipe:hacked emergency alert + alien language + viral prophecy + World Cup match + social media panic + UAP disclosure climate.The real story may not be aliens.It may be how easy it is to manufacture an alien narrative at national scale.  The killer framing  The emergency alert was real. The alien invasion was not. The psychological effect was the payload. Or: You do not need aliens to create an alien event. You need timing, trust infrastructure, screenshots and a scared audience. Or: Blue Beam is probably the wrong answer. But the public nervous system just got pinged. Or:  This was not an invasion. It was an intrusion into trust.  The most important correction  Do not write:  “Authorities later claimed the emergency broadcast system was hacked.”  Better:  “Authorities say a probable cyberattack or unauthorized remote access triggered false alerts, and the Federal Police and Anatel are investigating.”  That wording is stronger because it does not imply the official explanation is automatically false. It leaves room for investigation.  Also avoid: “Millions of Brazilians received an alert declaring an alien invasion had begun.” Better: “Millions received false emergency alerts; some messages reportedly included alien-invasion language, while others used ‘misantropia’ or disaster-style warnings.” That matters because the exact wording varied by location and channel.  The strongest missing point: this is a trust-infrastructure attack  The obvious story is “alien alert.” The real story is public trust sabotage.  Emergency alerts are different from normal social media posts. They are designed to override your phone, make a noise, and demand immediate action. When that channel is corrupted, the harm is not just confusion that night. The harm is future doubt. The best line:  Every false alert spends trust that may be needed during a real disaster.  Brazil has floods, landslides, storms and climate-risk events. If people learn that “extreme alert” can mean “hacker meme,” the next real warning may get ignored.  Suggested post addition:  The most dangerous part is not that people briefly wondered about aliens. The dangerous part is that a life-safety channel was turned into a prank or psychological operation. The next time Defesa Civil warns about a flood, fire, landslide or extreme weather, some people may hesitate. That hesitation can kill.  The Operation Blue Beam angle — how to handle it without sounding gullible  Project/Operation Blue Beam is commonly described as a 1990s conspiracy theory associated with Serge Monast, alleging a staged supernatural or alien event using advanced technology to manipulate humanity into a new world order.  The problem with invoking Blue Beam too quickly is that it turns a real, testable cyber incident into an unfalsifiable master plan. A stronger version is:  This does not prove Blue Beam. But it does prove that Blue Beam-style narratives can now be triggered with ordinary tools: emergency alerts, viral predictions, deepfakes, AI images, fake official screenshots, drone swarms, hacked accounts and social media amplification.  That is the genius-level point. You do not need to believe in the full Blue Beam theory to recognize that the Blue Beam-shaped vulnerability exists.  Better framing: “Blue Beam as metaphor, not conclusion”  Use this:  Operation Blue Beam may not be the literal explanation. But as a metaphor for staged reality, institutional distrust, artificial spectacle and mass psychological priming, the Brazil incident is exactly the kind of event that makes the theory go viral.  That lets you discuss the resonance without claiming proof.  The hidden strategic angle: “attention capture”  The alien wording may not be random. It is the most attention-maximizing text imaginable.  A false alert saying “test error” gets local attention. A false alert saying “alien invasion” goes global. A false alert timed near a viral World Cup abduction prophecy becomes memetic fuel.  Suggested line:  If the goal was attention, the attacker chose the highest-virality payload available.  That does not prove a coordinated UAP psyop. It may simply prove the attacker understood the internet.  The psychic prediction should be handled carefully  The prophecy does not become credible because a hack happened. The hack does not become paranormal because a prophecy went viral.  Separate them:  The psychic prediction is a viral cultural object. The alert hack is a cybersecurity event. The World Cup match is a scheduled mass-attention event. The Blue Beam theory is an interpretive overlay.The danger is when people collapse all four into one certainty.  Suggested line:  Coincidence plus virality is not causation. But coincidence plus virality can still move crowds.  Stronger “what would prove coordination?” checklist  To move from “weird timing” to “coordinated operation,” you would need evidence like: access logs showing who triggered the alerts; IP addresses, infrastructure, malware or credentials used; whether the attackers referenced the psychic prediction internally; whether alert text was drafted before or after the prophecy went viral; whether bot networks amplified both stories; whether foreign or domestic influence networks pushed the connection; whether the same accounts seeded the prophecy, the alert screenshots and Blue Beam interpretation; whether copycat alerts or stadium hoaxes are planned for June 24; whether any official or contractor system was compromised beyond the alert platform.  Best line:  The bridge between coincidence and operation is logs.  Genius-level solution: treat June 24 as a public-information security event  Do not just speculate. Propose readiness.  For the Brazil vs Scotland match in Miami, authorities, FIFA, broadcasters and platforms should prepare for:  fake emergency alerts; fake stadium evacuation messages; deepfake “official” videos; AI-generated UFO footage; drone or balloon hoaxes; edited crowd-panic clips; fake police scanner audio; fake FIFA statements; fake Brazil/Scotland team alerts; bot-driven “abduction confirmed” trends; spoofed livestream overlays; panic-buying or venue-rush rumors; misinformation in Portuguese, English and Spanish.  Suggested line:  The June 24 risk may not be aliens. It may be a hoax designed to make millions think aliens arrived.  The emergency-alert security demands  This is the policy-heavy part that makes the post useful.  Brazil should publish, after investigation:  who had credentials to issue alerts; whether MFA was enforced; whether test and production environments were separated; whether alerts required dual authorization; whether geographic targeting controls failed; whether there was an audit trail; whether the attacker used stolen credentials or system vulnerability; why 10 alerts could be sent before shutdown; why WhatsApp/SMS/Cell Broadcast channels differed; why Google Maps reportedly incorporated false official alerts; what changes will prevent recurrence.  Suggested line:  A false alien alert is funny for five minutes. A compromised civil-defense pipeline is a national-security issue.  The Google Maps propagation point  Agência Brasil and Folha reported that official public-alert data can flow into third-party systems such as Google public alerts/Maps. Folha reported Google updated Maps after false Defesa Civil alerts were displayed because its public-alert system reflects official government alert feeds.  That is a huge missing element.  The modern alert ecosystem is not one channel. It is: government alert platform; cell broadcast; SMS; WhatsApp or local channels; Google public alerts; Maps; screenshots; news; social media; AI summaries; influencers. Best line:  A hacked official alert does not stay inside the alert system. It becomes data that other platforms may amplify.  Obscure but powerful thought inputs  1. Blue Beam no longer needs holograms. The internet is the projector. Screenshots are the sky.  2. The alien message was a trust exploit. The attacker exploited not just software access, but the psychological authority of emergency warnings.  3. The payload was ambiguity. Nobody needed to convince everyone aliens were real. They only needed to make millions ask, “What if?”  4. The World Cup is a perfect myth amplifier. Global broadcast, national emotion, mass crowd, cameras everywhere, high stakes, instant translation, and millions watching for anomalies.  5. Brazil is uniquely fertile UFO terrain. Brazil has a deep UFO culture, famous cases, major skywatch traditions, spiritualist movements and a public already receptive to UAP stories. 6. The prophecy became infrastructure. A viral prediction gives later events a template. Without the prophecy, the alert is a hack. With the prophecy, the hack becomes “confirmation” to some audiences. 7. False alerts create future vulnerability. The next real emergency may be discounted because people remember the alien/misanthropy alert. 8. Debunking too aggressively can backfire. If officials only say “nothing happened,” conspiracy audiences hear “cover-up.” Better communication explains exactly what happened, what failed, what logs show and what is being fixed.  9. The first casualty is not truth. It is confidence. People may no longer know which alerts, videos, statements or screenshots are real.  10. The attacker may be measuring reaction. Even if this was a prank, the response data are valuable: who panicked, who amplified, which platforms spread it, how officials reacted, how fast narratives formed.  The best “what to watch next” list  Watch for:  official Federal Police findings; Anatel technical findings; whether the attacker used stolen credentials or platform vulnerability; whether any alert templates were preloaded; whether the alien wording came from one channel or multiple; whether the June 24 match triggers copycat hoaxes; whether fake FIFA/police/stadium statements circulate; whether drone sightings increase around Miami; whether accounts linking the prophecy and the alert show coordinated behavior; whether emergency alert systems in other countries face copycats.  Best “skeptical but open” paragraph I am not saying this is Blue Beam. The known facts support a false-alert/cybersecurity incident, not an alien operation. But dismissing it as “just a hack” also misses the point. A hacked emergency alert using alien-invasion language, days before a viral alien-abduction prophecy tied to Brazil’s World Cup match, is a perfect case study in how modern mass-perception events form. Best reply to “it was just a hack” “Just a hack” is not reassuring when the hacked system is a national emergency warning channel. The alien wording is funny; the security failure is not. Best reply to “this proves Blue Beam” It proves a Blue Beam-shaped vulnerability, not Blue Beam itself. Show the logs, infrastructure, coordination, amplification networks and intent before declaring an operation. Best reply to “the psychic predicted it” The psychic predicted an event at the June 24 Miami match. The false alerts happened in Brazil on June 19–20. That is not a match. It is a narrative bridge people are building after the fact. What not to say  Avoid:  “Brazilians were told an alien invasion had begun.”  Better:  “Millions received false emergency alerts; some included alien-invasion language, while others contained ‘misantropia’ or disaster-style warnings.”  Avoid:  “Authorities claimed it was hacked.” Better: “Authorities say a probable cyberattack or unauthorized remote trigger is under investigation by the Federal Police and Anatel.” Avoid: “This is Operation Blue Beam.” Better: “This is exactly the kind of trust-and-perception failure that makes Blue Beam narratives go viral.” Avoid: “The psychic predicted the alert.” Better: “A viral psychic prediction created the narrative frame that made the alert incident feel connected.” Avoid: “The World Cup alien abduction is coming.”  Better:  “June 24 is now a high-risk misinformation date.”  Best polished final post  Brazil just had a real emergency-alert incident collide with a viral alien prophecy — and that is the part people should be watching.Overnight from June 19 to June 20, Brazil’s Defesa Civil Alerta system sent false emergency alerts to millions of people across multiple states. Some messages reportedly included terms like “misantropia” and “invasão alienígena,” while local reports from Belo Horizonte described alerts saying “Proteja-se: ataque alienígena. Humanos, chegamos.”Authorities say the system was likely compromised, took the platform offline, and referred the incident to the Federal Police.On its own, that is already serious. Emergency alerts are supposed to be the most trusted signal a government can send. If that channel becomes hackable or memeable, the next real flood, landslide, fire or storm warning becomes less effective.But the timing is what made this explode online. A Brazilian spiritualist with a massive following recently went viral for predicting that UFOs would appear and abduct people at Brazil’s June 24 World Cup match in Miami. FIFA does have Scotland vs Brazil scheduled in Miami on June 24.That does not prove Operation Blue Beam.It proves the conditions for a mass perception event are already here: hacked alerts, alien language, a viral prophecy, a global football match, UAP anxiety, screenshots, memes and millions of people trying to decode the pattern in real time.The real question may not be “are aliens coming?”The real question is: who benefits from making people distrust official alerts, expect a spectacle, and interpret every anomaly through an alien-invasion script?Blue Beam may be the wrong answer. But the public nervous system just got pinged.  Best closing line  This was not an alien invasion. It was an invasion of the trust layer.

After reporting an international criminal network that worked online to groom and brainwash girls for human trafficking, my devices were hacked. I have been threatened not only to stay “silent”, but to “cooperate or die.” It’s crucial to indicate that the criminals were cyberstalking me online through all of my platforms, before I started my investigation. It was like online surveillance, however I’m here in Saudi Arabia. They asked me to contact them back and “to stay silent”. At the beginning, my husband and I took the threats lightly. But when our devices were hacked like a cyberattack, even our kid’s devices, we started contacting the Saudi authorities. As foreigners, we come to Saudi Arabia rooted in our memories and preparing for a future that’s far away from Saudi Arabia. And the rule is “not to mingle that much, not to be involved in any Saudi matter”,  just to “stay safe.”  The only real connection is our financial plans, to have a better future somewhere else.  When the criminal syndicate threatened me and my family “to cooperate” in illegal activities that violate all the rules of Saudi Arabia, to betray the country and work against its people, I couldn’t keep the common rule of “not to be involved in any Saudi matter.” I had to contact Saudi authorities, and I had to cooperate with Saudis for the safety of family and to reveal the motives of the perpetrators. Above all, I had to trust the Saudi authorities and the Saudi people whom I live among them. During COVID 19, I trusted Saudi doctors and a Saudi  female young nurse to keep me safe. Also, my family trusted a Saudi brother to deliver our stuff home during the pandemic, the quarantine difficult days! This time too, I had to trust. And I had to understand the society that I lived in as a stranger to be able to solve that crime puzzle and to make the right decisions. Only then, everything clicked into the right place.  I went to the Saudi authorities as a respectable researcher, but I wasn’t known as a credible journalist that time. Also, there were gaps in my research and many facts that were not obvious that time. The experiment was very hard, unfair, and sometimes xenophobic. But some Saudi officials, officers, and I’m certain of it, valued my sincerity and my knowledge. To them, I’m grateful!  The criminals built their coercive plan on the fact that I’m a foreigner, a woman, and a mama with a kid in her hand—a vulnerable target!  More importantly, this evil terrorism can’t work with a person who trusts the officials, the authorities.  The criminals aimed at exploiting vulnerable targets in the name of official institutions, disguising in official personas, to undermine the trust in institutions, create chaos, and to incite fear and destabilization. After thorough investigation and analysis, I was able to fill the gaps in my research, break the codes of the criminals, analyze the data, and reveal their motives.  And I reported the crime to His Excellency, HRH Abdulaziz bin Saud bin Nayef, the Minister of Interior, whom I trust. Finally, I published my investigation on Twitter and on the OSCE website.  Let the criminals know that this is what happens when they ask an honorable teacher-mama “to cooperate” in heinous crimes! She gives the criminals to the REAL COPS! And a simple mama from Alexandria, Egypt reached out to HRH the Minister of Interior to deliver an investigation and a complaint against the perpetrators.  Now, my investigation process stops here, as I delivered the report to the Saudi and Egyptian authorities to apply the rule of law and to keep my family safe from the criminal network’s revenge attacks.  Thank you!

The case for the shutdown: export controls on weapons have existed for decades. A model that can generate bioweapon or cyberattack guidance on demand is closer to a weapons system than productivity software. If you want AI regulated, accept that regulation sometimes cuts deep.

BREAKING: Brazil says 'probable' cyberattack sent false mobile phone emergency alerts to public  Context: Hackers breached Brazil’s national emergency alert system on June 20, 2026, sending fake “extreme alert” messages with the word “misantropia” (misanthropy) to millions of phones nationwide.  Why it matters: It exposes dangerous vulnerabilities in life-saving public warning systems, risking panic, eroded trust, and future alert fatigue.

Cybershield #44  🦅🔥  Captain’s Log – Loose Posts Sink Ships  The Nigerian Navy put it plainly: “Loose Posts Sink Ships.” Unauthorised disclosure of operational or security information through social media is an offence. As seafarers, one innocent photo of your vessel, route details, or crew activity can give attackers or pirates exactly what they need. I’ve seen cases where position leaks via social media led to real threats in the Gulf of Guinea.  Bridge Reflection Cybersecurity is not just about firewalls and passwords — it’s also about OpSec (Operational Security). What we post matters.  Three Shields Every Captain Must Enforce: 1.  Think before you post — no location, no sensitive photos, no operational details. 2.  Train crew on social media risks (even private groups aren’t safe). 3.  Report any suspicious requests for ship information immediately.  Captains & Seafarers — what’s one rule you follow before posting anything from sea? Drop it below. Tag 3 captains or maritime pros who need to see #44. Let’s keep our operations secure. The voyage continues. Stay sharp. Stay silent when necessary. Stay shielded. 🛡️🔐 #Cybershield #MaritimeCyberSecurity #GulfOfGuinea #OpSec #NigerianNavy #NigerianSeafarers #CyberAttack

Treat matches as potential IoCs and review them against normal admin activity.

sample_tweets.text: AIエージェントのセキュリティ判断を、人材不足の現場で自走させられるか——その材料が6月20日に動いた。  ai.whytrend.jp/x/e1f7d217f58…  【数値】GitHub Trendingで1日+336★、総16,885★へ急上昇  有志が公開したセキュリティ技能集が1日で336件のスターを集め、GitHubトレンド入りした。中身は「上級分析者がどの場面でどの手法を使い、何を前提確認し、結果をどう検証するか」という判断手順を754個に構造化したもの。攻撃コードのリポジトリではなく、分析者の思考手順そのものをエージェントに渡せる点が従来と違う。  ■ 754個の技能が動かす現場コスト  ・26分野754個の技能を1リポジトリに集約。概要走査を約30トークンに抑えた設計で、エージェントの文脈枠（処理できる情報量の上限）を圧迫せず全技能を一度に探索できる。 ・Claude Code・Cursor・GitHub Copilotなど26以上の開発支援ツールでそのまま使える。既存の環境に追加するだけで、定型のセキュリティ分析をエージェントに委ねる試行が始められる。  ■ コンプライアンス対応との接続——得する組織と確認が要る組織  ・全754技能をMITRE ATT&CK（攻撃手法の分類体系）、NIST CSF 2.0（サイバーセキュリティの国際標準枠組み）、NIST AI RMF（AIリスク管理の枠組み）など5基準に対応付け済み。社内のコンプライアンス文書と照合すれば、技能と監査根拠を同じ基準で紐づけられる。 ・ただし本プロジェクトはAnthropic公式ではなく独立した有志による制作。Apache 2.0ライセンスで商用利用は可能だが、継続的なサポートや保証は自前で評価する必要がある。公式調達を前提とする組織は、導入前にメンテナンス体制の確認を先に行う。  ■ 誰の判断コストが動くか  ・セキュリティ運用チーム: 上級分析者の判断手順を後付けでエージェントに組み込めるため、人材不足の中で定型分析の自走化を試せる。社内報告の根拠も5基準への対応表でそのまま示せる。 ・Claude Code・Cursor利用中の開発チーム: 既に使っているツールに1分野だけ読み込ませる小さな試行から始められる。導入コストの入口が低い。 ・公式サポートを調達条件とする組織: 有志制作という性質上、ベンダー保証を前提とした調達フローには乗らない。評価コストを別途見積もる必要がある。  ■ 次の一手——48時間以内に確認・照合・試す3点  ・確認する: リポジトリのApache 2.0ライセンス条文と、直近の更新頻度・コントリビューター体制を確認し、自社の継続利用に耐えるか判断する。 ・照合する: 自社が準拠するNIST CSF 2.0やNIST AI RMFの対象範囲と、754技能の対応表が社内コンプライアンス文書とどこまで重なるかを照合する。 ・試す: 既に使うClaude CodeやCursorに1分野の技能だけ読み込ませ、概要走査30トークンで文脈枠が圧迫されないかを実測する。  詳細な技能構造・5基準の対応表・導入手順は記事本文で。👇

自分用に調べたので、日本のCSIRTメンバーに共有  - mukul975 氏作のサイバーセキュリティAIスキル集。754個のSOC/DFIR/脅威インテリ実務手順書(SKILL.md)＋1,041本の参照用Pythonヘルパーで構成 - MITRE ATT&CK / NIST CSF など主要5フレームワークにマッピング、Claude Code等で利用可能  【公式性・信頼性】 - Anthropic公式ではない（名前にAnthropicが入るが README に「Not affiliated with Anthropic PBC」と明記。詐称ではなくコミュニティプロジェクトと正直に表示） - スター17.1k / Fork 2.1k / Apache-2.0 / 直近も活発に更新。作者は2018年からのアカウントでセキュリティ専攻、一定の信頼性あり - 軽セキュリティ監査では悪性コード（任意実行・難読化・認証情報窃取・外部送信）は未検出。スクリプトは自動実行ではなく参照素材  【既存スキルとの重複・差分】 - ほぼ重複なし。既存の security-audit / vuln-scan / threat-model 等は「自分のコードを監査する」プロセス系 - 本リポジトリはマルウェア解析・脅威ハンティング等の実務ドメイン知識系で、用途は補完的  【導入是非の結論】条件付き推奨（フルコピーは非推奨、選択導入を推奨） - 理由1: 754スキル全部の導入はコンテキスト肥大・トークン浪費。通常業務とは大半が無関係 - 理由2: ただしSOC/DFIR実務が発生した時の参照知識としては価値が高く品質も良い - 推奨運用: git clone で参照用に置き、必要な数スキルだけ手元の skills/ に取り込む。導入前に対象スクリプトを必ず目視確認（非公式のため自己責任）

Google DeepMind just published their AI Control Roadmap. The framing should disturb you: they treat internal AI agents as potential insider threats. The same way a company treats a rogue employee who already has office access.  The roadmap uses the MITRE ATT&CK framework, originally built for tracking human adversary tactics, to model what an AI agent might do if it goes off track. Trusted AI supervisors constantly review the working agent's reasoning, actions, and plans. If the supervisor sees something harmful, it blocks the action before damage occurs.  For low-risk actions, they review transcripts after the fact. For high-risk actions like major cyber attacks, they block in real time. Detection levels D1 through D4. Prevention levels R1 through R3.  Meanwhile, a new paper called Aegis proposes cryptographic runtime governance for autonomous AI. Each agent gets bound to an Immutable Ethics Policy Layer at system genesis. Policy-violating actions are not discouraged or flagged. They are rendered operationally non-executable. Verified violations trigger autonomous shutdown and generate auditable proof artifacts.  The binary signal: the industry has moved from how do we align AI to how do we contain AI that we know is imperfectly aligned. The assumption is no longer that alignment works. The assumption is that alignment will fail and we need defense in depth when it does.  The question is not whether AI will go off track. The question is whether you catch it before or after the damage.

If you lived at that time, would you have discerned that Jesus was Messiah?  I mean before His execution.  Or would you have believed the leadership accusing him of blasphemy, sabbath-breaking, consorting with lowlifes and Samaritans, and engaging in witchcraft?  He was Jesus of NAZARETH, but Messiah was supposed to come from the city of David, Bethlehem.  His mother conceived him out of wedlock. He probably looked very different from Joseph, meaning that his mother was a... you know.  In his entourage were a trained assassin, a TAX COLLECTOR, a bunch of ignorant fishermen, and women. "Service" women.  He was thrown out of his own synagogue for blasphemy. He encouraged his followers to break Shabbat by harvesting grain along the wayside. He defied the Sanhedrin and excoriated wise and learned men, calling them "whited sepulchres," full of corruption.  He refused to punish an adulteress. He claimed to be the Son of God — the Way, the Truth, and the Life. He claimed to fulfill the Law! he claimed to BE the law! He claimed to be the Lawgiver Himself!  He engaged in necromancy by raising Lazarus.  Do you see how everything that is familiar to us now was scandalous back then? Do you see how ordinary people might have heard such awful accusations about Him from people they trusted that they wouldn't have even LISTENED to Him?  Do you see how everything that is familiar to us now was scandalous back then? Do you see how ordinary people might have heard such awful accusations about Him from people they trusted that they wouldn't have even LISTENED to Him?  Imagine what people said about Christians while Rome tried to exterminate them! They would have said "These people follow a dead charlatan who blasphemed his way into execution, and then his followers stole his body from the tomb and claim he was resurrected like some god!"  If nasty, false, disgusting things can be said — and widely believed — of the Son of God, a perfect, sinless man, how much easier is it to smear a mere mortal like Joseph Smith?  Neo-religions were popping up like mushrooms during the early 1800s in NE U.S. Some were even weirder — and explicitly kinkier — than the "Mormons."   Those groups might have been mocked or denounced, but they weren't persecuted to the degree my ancestors were. Not even close. Early church members were chased out of upstate NY even though there was no polygamy even spoken about let alone practiced.   Then they were chased out of Ohio at gunpoint, then Missouri, then Illinois. Even after decamping to the middle of nowhere, the State still came after us.  That's WAY over the top. Way out of proportion to what my ancestors were doing and saying.  Look, if God establishes a Church, the Adversary is going to stir people up to murderous rage against it. Lies and distortions and slanders will surround it.  You can definitely argue that persecution doesn't prove divine origin, but at the same time it doesn't rule it out. On the contrary, it's a positive sign.  Especially given that we are highly productive, pro-social, law-abiding, service-oriented, family centered, trustworthy people.   "Oh, but that's to hide the demonic core."  Really? Good fruit coming from a corrupt tree?  I should think that if you find a much-slandered group that is actually well-behaved, you should pay attention.  Maybe you can discern divine influence by seeing whom the devil hates most.

Stuxnet was first publicly identified in 2010, but it had already been running inside Iran's Natanz enrichment facility for roughly two to three years before anyone outside the operation knew it existed. That gap matters. The weapon worked in silence, and the silence was part of the design.  The technical architecture is worth understanding in full, because the doctrine followed from it. Stuxnet targeted Siemens programmable logic controllers governing Iranian centrifuges, issuing commands that caused the centrifuges to physically destroy themselves while simultaneously feeding operators false readings showing normal performance. The machines were tearing themselves apart while the instruments said everything was fine. That combination, physical destruction plus sensor spoofing, was not an accident of engineering. It was a strategic choice about how to maximize damage while minimizing the window for response. Four separate zero-day vulnerabilities. Self-replicating propagation. Precision targeting logic designed to contain collateral spread. Industrial control system-specific payload. No one had built anything like it before, at least not anything that became publicly known.  The United States and Israel developed it jointly. That has never been formally acknowledged by either government, but it has also never been seriously disputed. The New York Times reporting in 2012, drawing on sources with direct knowledge, established the outline. NPR's June 2026 analysis treats it as established baseline, which it is.  What the NPR piece is doing, sixteen years after public discovery, is asking what Stuxnet actually built. The answer is uncomfortable.  The proof of concept Stuxnet demonstrated was this: a nation-state could cause physical destruction to an adversary's strategic infrastructure using only software, with plausible deniability, without a single soldier crossing a border, without a missile or a bomb, and with effects precise enough to avoid the kind of obvious provocation that triggers a conventional military response. That combination had never existed before. Once it was demonstrated, the logic was available to everyone.  Iran's response is the clearest direct line. After Natanz, Iran invested heavily in offensive cyber capabilities. This is documented. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Cyber Command grew substantially. Iranian APT groups, variously designated by researchers as APT33, APT34, APT35, and others, developed capabilities across espionage, disruption, and destructive attack. The Shamoon malware that hit Saudi Aramco in 2012, destroying data on roughly 30,000 workstations, came roughly two years after Stuxnet's public exposure. Iranian attribution to Shamoon has been widely assessed, though Tehran denied it. The timing and the context are not coincidental.  Russia had its own trajectory, but the Stuxnet precedent validated a doctrine Russian military planners had been developing conceptually. The attacks on Ukrainian power infrastructure in December 2015 and December 2016, using BlackEnergy and Industroyer respectively, were the operational expression of a Stuxnet-derived logic applied to a different target set. Lights went out in parts of Ukraine. The attackers had persistent access to industrial control systems. The operations were calibrated to cause disruption without triggering a full military response from NATO. That calibration, that space between demonstrable harm and the threshold of conventional war, is exactly the space Stuxnet proved existed.  China's approach has been somewhat different in emphasis. Chinese APT groups, including the cluster now tracked as Volt Typhoon, have been documented engaging in what U.S. intelligence has described as pre-positioning: achieving persistent access to American critical infrastructure including water systems, power grids, and communications networks, not for immediate exploitation but for potential wartime activation or coercive leverage. The CISA and NSA advisory on Volt Typhoon issued in 2024 was unusually direct about the assessed intent. This is not espionage infrastructure. It is sabotage infrastructure held in reserve. The concept of holding that kind of access, positioning it quietly for later use, descends directly from the Stuxnet model.  North Korea's cyber program is more financially motivated in its primary focus, but it has demonstrated destructive capability when directed. The Sony Pictures attack in 2014. WannaCry in 2017, which the U.S. UK, and others formally attributed to the Lazarus Group in 2018 and which caused somewhere between four and eight billion dollars in global damage. The destructive capacity is real and it has been used.  The proliferation dynamic NPR is documenting in 2026 is this: a capability that took extraordinary resources to develop in 2007 is now within reach of any state with a serious cyber investment and a few years of sustained effort. The technical barrier has dropped. The doctrine has spread. And the infrastructure for what the analysis calls "pre-positioned deterrence" now exists across multiple competing powers simultaneously.  The original rationale for Stuxnet, as best as can be reconstructed from reporting, was to buy time. The Natanz centrifuges were enriching uranium. The alternative being weighed was an Israeli airstrike, with all the escalatory risk that carried. A covert operation that set back the program by months or years without triggering a war looked like the better option. NPR frames it as a weapon that may have prevented a conventional military strike. That framing is defensible.  The cost of that choice is what the June 2026 analysis is actually about. The same logic that made Stuxnet attractive, deniable, precise, below the threshold of open war, made it a template that adversaries would inevitably adopt and adapt. Every state actor with serious capabilities now holds some version of this in reserve. The targets include power grids, water treatment, financial systems, communications infrastructure. The access has in many documented cases already been achieved. What has not happened yet, in most cases, is the decision to activate it.  That is the world Stuxnet built. Not a world where one country's centrifuges spun out of control in 2008. A world where multiple major powers have pre-positioned destructive access inside each other's critical systems and are making continuous calculations about whether the conditions for use have arrived.  The deterrence logic here is genuinely unstable in ways that nuclear deterrence, for all its horrors, is not. Nuclear weapons require physical delivery. The infrastructure for cyber pre-positioning is already inside the target. The action required to activate it is a software command. The attribution window can be narrow or disputed. The escalation ladder is not well-defined. There is no established treaty framework, no inspections regime, no red phone dedicated to this category of conflict. There have been bilateral cyber discussions between the U.S. and China, and there are norms conversations happening in various multilateral forums, but nothing binding, nothing with teeth.  Sixteen years from public discovery, Stuxnet's most durable legacy is not the centrifuges it destroyed. Those were eventually rebuilt. The legacy is the doctrine it proved, and the infrastructure that doctrine has since generated across every major state actor in the system.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #MalwareWarfare #IndustrialSabotage #ZeroDayExploitation #CriticalInfrastructureMapping #InfrastructureAttacks #CyberCapabilityDevelopment

In 2007, North Korea stood up a hacking unit that would eventually steal more than a billion dollars in a single year. The organizational decision to do it was made in 2003, after watching the U.S. invasion of Iraq. Pyongyang drew the obvious lesson: conventional military competition with the United States was a dead end. Cyber was the asymmetric play.  The unit that became Lazarus Group was built as a subordinate element under Lab 110, itself nested within Bureau 121 of the Reconnaissance General Bureau. The RGB is North Korea's primary foreign intelligence service, and Bureau 121 has long been identified as its main cyber arm. Recent intelligence analysis suggests Lab 110 is probably a reorganized, expanded version of Bureau 121 rather than a separate entity sitting above it. The hierarchy beneath Lab 110 includes TEMP.Hermit, APT38, and Andariel as distinct subordinate units with their own operational mandates. This isn't one team of hackers. It's a structured organization with divisions.  The groundwork went back further than 2007. North Korea started sending computer scientists abroad in the 1990s to acquire technical knowledge, then brought them home. By the early 2000s, the regime had made cyberwarfare a top institutional priority. The 2007 formalization of Lazarus Group was the organizational crystallization of that investment.  Initial activity focused on malware development and reconnaissance. The first significant public demonstration came in July 2009 with Operation Troy, which hit at least 39 websites using a botnet of somewhere between 20,000 and 166,000 hijacked machines. The range on that number reflects how hard attribution was even for relatively blunt operations. That difficulty was part of the point. North Korea had correctly assessed that the international framework for attributing and responding to cyberattacks was still being invented, and that an early mover with deniable capabilities could cause serious damage with limited consequence.  Operation Troy established the operational continuity that ran through DarkSeoul in 2013, the Sony Pictures hack in 2014, and the WannaCry ransomware campaign in 2017. Each operation was different in target and method. What they shared was organizational persistence. The same institutional structure kept producing attacks across a decade and a half.  By 2013, high-profile defectors were telling intelligence communities that North Korea had approximately 6,000 trained hackers. That figure, if accurate, represents a substantial national commitment to a single capability, particularly for a country of North Korea's size and economic constraints. Current assessments place the DPRK's cyber sophistication in the same general tier as China and Russia. That's a significant thing to say about a country that in most other military-technical domains is frozen somewhere around the 1970s.  The financial dimension is where the modern operation becomes hard to look away from. DPRK cyber actors stole at least $1.19 billion in 2024. One year. The proceeds move through cryptocurrency and get used to procure military equipment, funding a sanctions-constrained weapons program through digital theft. The regime has built a revenue stream that doesn't require agricultural exports, diplomatic concessions, or Chinese goodwill. It requires servers and skilled people.  The IT worker scheme runs alongside the straight theft. North Korean nationals use stolen identities to get hired at American companies, over 100 of them by recent Justice Department count. The Justice Department has seized 29 laptop farms across 16 states. These aren't sophisticated covers. In many cases, someone in the U.S. is physically operating the laptop and maintaining the fiction of a domestic employee. The wages go back to Pyongyang. The access goes to intelligence collection or future intrusion.  The Lazarus Group formation in 2007 is worth marking precisely because it was so deliberate. This wasn't a capability that emerged from freelancers or opportunistic criminal enterprise that the state later co-opted. It was a planned institutional response to a strategic problem, built from the ground up inside an intelligence service, staffed by people the state had spent years training abroad. The decision to build it preceded the formal stand-up by four years. The returns on that decision have compounded for nearly two decades.  Other states watched and drew conclusions. The model, offensive cyber as a low-attribution, high-return asymmetric tool operated through a dedicated unit inside military intelligence, is not unique to North Korea anymore. But North Korea ran the proof of concept. The 2007 formation is where that particular experiment got serious.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CyberCapabilityDevelopment #MalwareDistribution

As the FIFA World Cup continues, soccer fans from around the world, including Israel, will be benefiting from the protection that Israeli companies have provided to aid authorities in ensuring that the largest sporting event in the world runs smoothly.  Israeli companies have been, and still are, involved in protecting the tournament against hackers, scammers, and other cyber threats, as well as the risk of drone attacks, coordinating with authorities on a local, national, and international level.  One such company, KELA Group, referred to the World Cup, which is ongoing in the US, Mexico, and Canada, as the “largest digital battlefield in history.” According  to KELA, the event features an “enormous digital attack surface,” as  approximately 6.5 million ticketed attendees are expected throughout the  weeks-long event, as well as a worldwide viewing audience, possibly  reaching six billion.  The  tournament, KELA says, relies on a “massively interconnected digital  supply chain involving third-party vendors, transportation, hospitality,  and cloud services.” This means that the failure of even a minor link  in the chain could disrupt core parts of the operations, the company  said. KELA  noted that geopolitics has an effect on state actors attempting to  disrupt events. This has been seen with Russian and Iranian threat  actors using intelligence and psychological warfare.  KELA  Group noted that in past events, Russian hacktivists, including APT28,  have focused on covert intelligence collection, while Storm-1679 used  AI-generated voices of celebrities to incite fear.  Meanwhile,  Iranian Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors have targeted critical  infrastructure, with the Israeli company highlighting that Handala wiped  over 200,000 systems at a US medical tech company, Stryker, and doxxed  (revealed personal information of) FBI Director Kash Patel. James Jenn, Jerusalem Post

The moment that triggered the Trump administration’s dramatic crackdown on Anthropic, and may completely reset the ground rules for U.S. regulation of AI, happened almost by accident. 

And it was sparked by one of Anthropic’s top investors: Amazon. 

Last week, researchers at Amazon were busy stress-testing Anthropic’s newly released Fable 5, a “safe” version of Anthropic’s Mythos AI model. Anthropic had repeatedly said that its Mythos-class models had superhuman software hacking skills that were too dangerous to be released to the general public. Fable 5, which Anthropic launched on June 9, was equipped with what it said were robust safeguards against cybersecurity risks. 

The Amazon researchers testing Fable, however, discovered a “jailbreak” that they documented as allowing users to bypass safety rails and access information that could be used in a cyberattack. Amazon promptly notified Anthropic. But the situation suddenly escalated when Amazon Chief Executive Andy Jassy happened to be on a pre-scheduled call with White House officials last Thursday, June 11, regarding an unrelated topic, according to two sources familiar with Amazon. Jassy brought up the vulnerability his team had found, and White House officials encouraged him to flag the issue directly to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, the sources said.

Jassy spoke to Bessent that same day. He told the treasury secretary about the Fable jailbreak but also stressed that he was concerned about the cyber capabilities of all of the frontier AI models, including those from other labs. Bessent has been leading the administration’s response to Anthropic’s Mythos model, largely because of the threat Mythos-powered cyberattacks could pose to the global financial system. His phone call with Jassy set in motion a chain of events that quickly became international news. 

By Friday evening, just four days after Fable’s launch, U.S. Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick had hit Anthropic with unprecedented export controls, forcing the lab to pull its most powerful models. The move marked the first time the U.S. government stepped in to explicitly limit the release of a frontier AI model, triggering a wave of tech and political alarm around the world. Since then, cybersecurity experts, AI policy thinkers, American officials, and foreign governments have fiercely debated the consequences, questioning whether the Trump Administration’s heavy-handed decision sets a dangerous precedent, or if it reflects wise caution. 

The stage for the watershed decision had been set months earlier, as the Trump administration wrangled with Anthropic over a separate issue involving its contract with the Pentagon, a fight that deepened a growing animosity toward the San Francisco AI company within the circle of tech policy officials in Trump’s orbit. It was further primed by the surging unease within national security agencies as Anthropic began expanding access to Mythos to an broader roster of companies.

For Anthropic, which recently closed a $65 billion funding round at a valuation of $965 billion and has filed paperwork to list its shares on the public markets, the export controls represent an existential threat at a time when rivals OpenAI, SpaceX, and Google are competing aggressively to dominate the AI business.

Many of the details of the weeklong showdown between Anthropic and the Trump administration are still emerging, and efforts to resolve the situation are fluid. Fortune spoke to more than a half dozen people involved on various sides of the situation to piece together how it happened and what it could mean going forward. 

An ultimatum, and a flurry of frantic calls

On Friday, following Jassy’s phone call with Bessent, the administration contacted Anthropic and Chief Executive Dario Amodei, demanding that they either fix the jailbreak or remove the models from the market. According to a source familiar with Anthropic, the warning arrived with no prior communication or indication from the government that it considered the export of Fable a national security threat. Despite the lack of warning, the Commerce Department issued a 90-minute deadline, followed by strict export controls banning foreign nationals from using the software, a directive that Anthropic interpreted as an effective ban on the models themselves.

That’s because the government uses a “deemed export” standard that interprets the distribution of an AI model to any foreign national, including those physically located in the U.S., as the equivalent of exporting the model. That standard would apply even to Anthropic’s own non-U.S. employees. What’s more, Anthropic had no effective way of verifying the nationality of its users. 

The ultimatum set off a flurry of frantic calls between Anthropic and the White House. Anthropic maintained that the jailbreak was relatively simple and could easily be achieved using other available models, the source said. But the argument failed to sway administration officials, who maintained that the bypass unlocked dangerous cyber capabilities. 

An official letter from Lutnick, whose department oversees export controls, arrived just after 5 p.m. Eastern Time on Friday, informing the company that it required government approval before making its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models available to any user abroad or any foreign national globally. Lutnick warned that non-compliance could result in criminal and civil penalties, according to a copy of the directive obtained by Bloomberg News.

By 10 p.m. that evening, Anthropic had taken Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline.

“We disagree that the finding of a narrow potential jailbreak should be cause for recalling a commercial model deployed to hundreds of millions of people,” the company wrote in its blog post after taking the models offline. “If this standard was applied across the industry, we believe it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.”

An Anthropic spokesperson added that both parties are working quickly to resolve the issue. Representatives for the White House did not respond to multiple requests for comment.

The saga continued into the weekend and this week. By Sunday, senior Anthropic technical staff had arrived in D.C. to meet White House officials in person, the source familiar with Anthropic said.

“The Admin values Anthropic’s technical capabilities and feels that this issue, while serious, should be easily resolved,” David Sacks, a venture capitalist who serves in an advisory role in the Trump Administration, wrote on X over the weekend. “The ball is in Anthropic’s court.”

But by Tuesday, Fortune understands, those talks had concluded with the export controls still in place and the next steps unclear. The two sides reached an impasse.

Industry blowback

While the immediate future of Fable remains in limbo, many cybersecurity experts have come to Anthropic’s defense. They warn that government licensing of AI models will make U.S. labs less competitive and allow geopolitical rivals like China to swoop in. 

“Anything that stops the evolution of technology and development of technology is just going to leave the U.S. behind,” Danny Jenkins, chief executive at cybersecurity company ThreatLocker, told Fortune. “Criminals don’t care whether they’re allowed to export something from the U.S. or not.”

At a demo this week, Jenkins said he used Anthropic’s Claude model, which is not considered as potent as Fable or Mythos, to “completely ransomware a machine,” which the model itself eventually identified as textbook ransomware. But, Jenkins said, vulnerabilities are going to be exposed eventually, whether by a human or an AI model. The AI model simply does it faster. He suggested the government’s focus should be on hardening security and creating “zero trust” environments, rather than trying to police a model’s existence.

Legal experts have also questioned the logic of the export controls. Charlie Bullock, a senior research fellow on LawAI’s U.S. Law and Policy team, told Fortune that Lutnick’s letter leans on authorities that were never designed for publicly accessible AI systems, especially where users only interact with a model via an API (a software interface that lets different programs communicate). 

That distinction raises questions about whether the government has stretched its statutory powers and whether the move can withstand First Amendment scrutiny, since earlier court decisions have treated published computer code as a form of protected speech. 

“I think the takeaway is that the export control letter is on very shaky legal ground,” Bullock said.

Since first flagging the issue to White House officials, Amazon has taken a step back on negotiations between Anthropic and the U.S. government. Amazon is one of Anthropic’s largest investors, having invested at least $13 billion in the startup in a series of deals that date back to 2023.

One engineer at Amazon said that employees have been making jokes that their company “snitched” on Anthropic, while others lamented the fact that they never had an opportunity to use Fable 5.

In a statement, an Amazon spokesperson said that as one of the world’s largest cloud providers, “it’s not uncommon for governments to seek our counsel on potential security risks. When they occur, we don’t share the details of these discussions.” 

While it’s unclear whether Jassy expected his comments to White House officials to result in the export controls, it’s almost certain that he would have been aware of the tension between Anthropic and the Trump administration.

International reverberations

Back in October, Sacks, who used to serve as the Trump AI and crypto czar, lashed out at Anthropic for advocating for AI regulation at both the federal and state level, accusing the company of running a “sophisticated regulatory capture strategy based on fear-mongering.” He had previously accused the company of being “woke” and staffed with “leftists.”

This was followed by a highly publicized feud between the lab and the Trump Administration over the Defense Department’s use of Anthropic’s models. The Pentagon wanted Anthropic to agree to a new contract that would make it clear it could use the company’s models for “any lawful purpose,” while Anthropic wanted specific exemptions built into the contract that would bar its models from being used to control autonomous weapons or for domestic mass surveillance. When the two couldn’t come to terms, the Trump administration ordered all federal agencies to stop using Anthropic’s models and labeled Anthropic “a supply chain risk,” a designation that had never before been applied to a U.S. business, and which meant Anthropic’s models could not be used by any part of the U.S. military or by any defense contractors when working on Pentagon contracts. Anthropic has challenged the designation in court.

When Anthropic released Mythos in April, the relationship with the U.S. government appeared to be resetting on better footing. Because Mythos can find previously unknown security vulnerabilities—including in open source code bases that have been subject to extensive scrutiny by human security researchers for decades—and can then autonomously string these vulnerabilities together to run entire exploits, Anthropic coordinated with U.S. national security agencies. Anthropic’s Glasswing program initially released the model to a carefully curated group of 12 launch partners, all U.S. technology or financial firms whose systems controlled critical U.S. infrastructure. The idea was that these companies would use Mythos to find and patch vulnerabilities in their own software before hackers, who are increasingly using AI to find vulnerabilities and automate attacks, discovered them. Amazon and Microsoft were part of the initial group, which was then expanded to some 40 additional U.S. software companies in consultation with U.S. security agencies.

But the model’s debut also sparked panic among foreign governments and businesses, who were clamoring for access to Mythos to help them secure their code bases. Several weeks ago, Anthropic gave the U.S. government a list of 111 trusted organizations, many of them located overseas, that would also get access to Mythos. The administration approved this list. However, the lab later informed officials it had expanded the list by about 50 additional organizations, The Washington Post reported.

One of those new entries, the newspaper reported, was a South Korean telecommunications firm the administration suspected of having ties to China. A source close to Anthropic said the company did receive a request from the U.S. government several weeks ago to cut off Mythos access to a global telecommunications company. The source said Anthropic did this quickly after receiving the request. Other news accounts suggest the company was SK Telecom, although SK issued a statement saying “there is no basis for considering our company to be linked with China.”

The revelation of the ballooning Glasswing roster and the inclusion of the Korean telecom firm in that group damaged trust between the U.S. national security establishment and Anthropic, according to the Post. Even before Fable was rolled out last week, the National Security Agency was actively pushing the administration to put emergency export controls in effect, the report said.

Can the rift be repaired?

Amodei reportedly spent last weekend working the phones, trying in vain to persuade the administration to reverse its decision, while the company prepared to send its delegation of technical experts to Washington. According to press accounts, government officials were insisting Anthropic create guardrails for Fable that can’t be bypassed. But many experts in AI security say that all AI models are currently susceptible to some form of jailbreaking and that there is no known technical fix for this vulnerability.

The rift between Anthropic and the White House was on display Wednesday on the global stage at the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, where Amodei and Trump found themselves in the same room for a roundtable discussion of more than two-dozen business and government leaders. Seated directly at the president’s side was Sam Altman, Amodei’s arch-rival from OpenAI, who has remained publicly mum about the latest feud between the U.S. and Anthropic.

Across the room, on the other side of a large oval table, Amodei sat next to French President Emmanuel Macron. Addressing the group, the Anthropic CEO reportedly offered a pointed warning: “resist the temptation to splinter” in how they handle AI regulation.

On Thursday, Politico reported that Anthropic and the U.S. government were working on a deal to restore access to Fable and Mythos that would also see them jointly develop a framework for judging when future jailbreaks of a model’s guardrails posed a serious danger.

Meanwhile, in Seoul, South Korea, Chris Cauri, Anthropic’s managing director of international, told reporters at a press conference that “we are very confident that in the coming days, the models will be available again.”

But, as of Thursday evening Pacific Time, exactly how that would happen remained a mystery.

This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

BYD도 'UN R155' 같은 국제 사이버보안 규제를 맞추며 한국 진출을 준비 중이지만, 무선 연결(OTA) 통로가 열려있다는 것 자체가 해커에겐 가장 매력적인 타겟입니다.  편의성을 얻은 대가로 우리는 '달리는 해킹 위험'을  떠안게 된 셈입니다.  기능이 화려해질수록 차를 살 때 '보안 등급'부터 따져야 하는 날이 머지않았습니다.  무선 업데이트되는 차, 100% 신뢰하고 타실 수 있나요?  📌 나중에 차 고를 때 참고하도록  북마크 해두세요.

미국이 직접 조치를 시작할때는 단순한 한미공조 수사가 아니라 미국을 주축으로 부정선거에 피해를 입은 국가들이 연합한 국제 합동 부정선거 수사단을 출범시켜 국제적으로 압박해 들어올 가능성이 높습니다.   아래는 가상 시나리오로 AI에 물어본 내용입니다. ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡ  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

sample_tweets.text: Jake 9/11 took decades of planning, coordination, and billions to execute.   They wish they could slap together another attack like that.  The most likely threat is Trumps assassination. Which would be blamed on Iran.  Or a cyber attack against critical banking/energy.
9/11 took decades to plan and execute. Can't be so easily done again.   But, simply shooting Trump and blaming Iran would have a similar effect in their minds.  That or a cyber attack.

The answer is: keep building more Burkes. That is the easy-button solution for the near term. But it is not the full solution for the 2030s through mid-century and beyond.  USNI reports that SASC appears to want both: another Flight III Burke multi-year procurement deal beginning in FY2028, while also keeping DDG(X) alive.  That makes sense.  The Navy should keep building Flight III Burkes to preserve shipyard rhythm, maintain momentum, retain crew familiarity, sustain Aegis continuity, and protect near-term fleet numbers.  But the Burke is not the long-term destination.  The Burke hull is a brilliant design, but Flight III is already carrying a huge load: SPY-6, modern Aegis, air and missile defense command, cruiser-replacement duties, and the same basic 96-cell VLS architecture.  The issue is not whether Burkes work.  They do.  The issue is whether the Burke hull has enough future margin for the next 30–40 years of: larger radars, more power, more cooling, directed-energy weapons, heavier electronic warfare, larger missiles, heavier CIWS layers, heavier guns, ballistic and hypersonic missile defense, space-enabled targeting networks, counter-drone saturation defense, and greater command capacity.  That is where DDG(X) starts to make sense.  Not because Burke is bad. Because Burke is too successful and too heavily loaded.  keep building Flight III Burkes as the bridge. But do not pretend the bridge is the destination.  DDG(X) is where the Navy tries to buy back margin.  The deeper challenge is not simply “Burke vs DDG(X) vs battleship.”  The real challenge is improving the entire shipbuilding system: more automation in ship production, more modularity, better digital ship design, cleaner upgrade pathways, more repeatable construction, more yards able to contribute, more industrial rhythm, and more production momentum.  America made mistakes to go to war for many years for the interests of others. Meanwhile, the adversaries all strengthened their autonomous factory systems.  Again America thinks about war from ship pint of view, war, and not from regenerative point of view, ship factory.  That is why DDG(X) matters more than a boutique low-quantity battleship program.  A few custom prestige ships may look impressive, but they do not fix the production base.  The priority should be a next-generation destroyer design that can scale across decades: more space, more power, more cooling, more weapons margin, better sensors, stronger self-defense, and easier modernization.  Burkes are the bridge.  DDG(X) is the chance to redesign the bridge, the factory, and the future fleet at the same time.  Economies of scale are required in order to justify new factory build out programs. This means destroyers, corvettes etc..  The Navy does not just need a bigger destroyer. It needs a better shipbuilding machine.  And the ships produced by that machine should be designed from the beginning to fight as a network.  The real combat effect is not only what one ship can do alone.  It is what many ships can do together when their sensors, weapons, command systems, drones, missiles, and decision loops are connected at scale.  That is why quantity matters.  A single giant ship gives you one powerful node.  A fleet of networked ships creates many possible sensor-shooter-command relationships.  Using a simple Metcalfe-style network valuation: 2 ships create 1 possible ship-to-ship connection. 4 ships create 6. 8 ships create 28. 16 ships create 120. 32 ships create 496.  That does not mean combat power magically becomes 496 times greater.  War is not that simple.  Network value can be degraded by bandwidth limits, electronic warfare, targeting gaps, magazine depth, doctrine, logistics, maintenance, and human command.  But the basic point holds:  a scalable destroyer fleet creates many more combinations of sensing, targeting, defending, striking, decoying, and command-sharing than a few boutique battleships.  This is why DDG(X) matters. That's why the network matters. The network swarm of DDG(X) is the real Battleship.  The Navy should not only ask: “How big can one ship be?”  It should ask: “How much fleet combat value does each additional hull add to the network?”  A low-number battleship program creates impressive individual platforms.  A scalable next-generation destroyer program creates exponentially compounding fleet value.  The exponential destroys all others.  The future is not just bigger ships or smaller ships or both.  It is better ships, built faster, upgraded easier, and networked so every additional hull increases the combat value of the whole force.  Note: Networking the Burke fleet is a great life-extension strategy.  Not because it magically solves every physical limit of the hull.  It does not.  A network cannot create infinite space, weight, power, cooling, magazine depth, or upgrade margin.  But networking can extend the combat relevance of the Burke fleet.  A Burke does not have to do everything alone if it is part of a larger sensor-shooter-command network.  One ship may sense. Another may shoot Another may defend. Another may command. Another may launch drones or decoys. Another may carry additional missile capacity.  That is how the existing Burke fleet can remain powerful for longer: not only as individual ships, but as connected combat nodes.  So more Burkes are the near-term bridge. Networked Burkes are the life-extension program. DDG(X) is the growth-margin platform for the next era.  The mistake would be choosing only one layer.  Unfortunately,  Even though burkes are highly network-capable, they are not yet fully optimized as a fleet-scale sensor-shooter-command mesh.  They already have major network warfare layers: Aegis, Link 16-type tactical data links, Cooperative Engagement Capability, NIFC-CA concepts, SPY-6 on Flight III, and integration with aircraft like E-2D/F-35 in some kill-chain architectures. CEC, for example, is described by the Navy as a real-time sensor-netting system that lets ships and aircraft share radar data to create a common track picture.  But “fully networked”  means something stronger:  Every Burke, aircraft, unmanned system, satellite layer, missile battery, and allied platform can reliably share sensing, targeting, command, deception, and engagement data under jamming, cyber attack, emissions control, degraded bandwidth, and combat losses.  That is not what exists as a clean universal reality.  The public evidence says the Navy has important pieces of the network, not a perfect all-domain mesh. Flight III Burkes add SPY-6 and stronger Integrated Air and Missile Defense capability, but DDG(X) is still being justified partly because the Navy wants more space, weight, power, cooling, integrated power, weapon capacity, and long-term growth margin than Flight III can provide.  Burkes are networked enough to remain dangerous, but not fully networked enough to be the final answer.   Maybe they will one day enable full feature set on Burke! And build the DDG(X) too!
